definition: Sell stops resting below swing lows, equal lows, or range lows where long-position traders place stop-losses. Smart money targets these pools to pair orders, often before a reversal or continuation. Sweep of SSL followed by aggressive displacement and market structure shift validates long entries; in bearish context, SSL serves as draw-on-liquidity destination and profit target.
identification_rules: Locate swing lows (lows with a higher low on each side) or equal lows (two or more lows at roughly the same price) on the relevant time frame. These levels contain resting sell stops from long positions.
Mark these levels as sell side liquidity if price has not yet traded below them. Also consider previous day low, previous week low, and session lows (Asia, London) as sell side liquidity pools.
When price is in a consolidation range, treat the range low (using candle bodies, not wicks) as the sell side liquidity target.

For bearish targets, confirm a bearish context: price should be reacting from a higher-timeframe PD array (order block, FVG, etc.) with displacement lower, or a change in state of delivery (CISD) has occurred.
To confirm a sweep, observe price trading below the identified low, ideally with a wick and a close back inside the prior range, or a clear take-out during expansion.
For reversal setups, a valid sweep must be followed by aggressive displacement and a market structure shift to the upside (e.g., price closing on the higher side, breaking structure).
Ensure the swept level is associated with a higher time frame important level (HTF FVG, prior order block, key swing low) for higher probability.
In the box setup, after an aggressive deviation above the range and an aggressive close back inside, the range low becomes the projected sell side liquidity target for shorts.
Combine with standard deviation projection tool to confirm -2 to -2.5 standard deviation levels align with the order cluster below swing low.
Check for confluence with other PD arrays (Order Block, Fair Value Gap) to enhance confirmation.
In bearish OTE scenario, drag Fibonacci from swing high to swing low; the stop cluster below swing low is the target SSL.
When price breaks below the low and closes back above the low, consider the sweep complete and update the level to the next valid swing low.
If current candle is directional (body larger than wick) and closes downward, prioritize expectation of price moving to SSL; if indecision candle (close near open), mark both sides liquidity.
After sweep, observe if price quickly recovers above the low (displacement and retracement) to confirm sweep validity rather than true breakdown.
trading_implication: Sell side liquidity acts as a downside draw and profit target for shorts, especially after a bearish change in state of delivery or rejection from a PD array, with entries taken at premium levels targeting SSL. A sweep of SSL followed by aggressive displacement higher validates long entry setups at discount levels (breaker blocks, order blocks, FVGs) with stops below the swept low. In range setups, SSL is targeted after aggressive deviations back into the range. It is fractal across timeframes, requiring minimum 2R targets.
common_mistakes: Treating a minor wick below a low as a valid sweep without waiting for a close back inside the range or aggressive displacement.
Entering long on a sweep without confirmation of aggressive displacement and a market structure shift to the upside.
Assuming every SSL sweep guarantees a reversal, ignoring that losses can still occur.
Targeting sell-side liquidity for shorts without first confirming a bearish change in state of delivery or bearish rejection from a PD array.
Treating any low sweep as SSL without associating it with a higher time frame important level (HTF FVG, prior order block, key swing low).
Marking wicks rather than candle bodies when defining swing lows / sell-side liquidity levels, or misidentifying swing lows by not confirming higher lows on both sides.
Overlooking equal lows and focusing only on single swing lows.
Ignoring higher time frame liquidity levels like previous day, week, or session lows.
Entering shorts toward SSL when the available reward is below 2R without projecting to further targets.
Looking for short setups in a range when the move back inside is not aggressive enough.
Placing stop-losses too tight above the swept low instead of beyond the swing low.
Expecting a move to sell-side liquidity without a confirmed bearish structural shift (e.g., an intermediate-term high).
Assuming price will retest a broken high instead of delivering to sell-side liquidity.
Sell Side Liquidity swept but not updating the level to the next valid swing low.
Only marking swing lows on a single timeframe, ignoring multi-tier SSL levels (previous day low, week low, session low).
When price is in consolidation (candle neither breaks high nor low), forcing expectation of price moving to SSL.
Entering long immediately after sweep without waiting for price to displace through Breaker Block, FVG, etc.
Treating price touching low as entry signal without waiting for close back inside range to confirm sweep.
Using sweep at arbitrary low without higher timeframe important level support.
Relying solely on SMT divergence or SSL sweep signal without higher timeframe bias and structure confirmation.

related_concepts.term: Change in the state of delivery

merge_notes: All three input cards are from ICT school; no school conflict. The Foundation card uses Chinese in definition but English in rules; translated to English. Nuance preserved: standard deviation projection from first card, box setup from second, OTE and directional candle from third.
